# Diabetic Kidney Disease

## Learning Objectives

1. Differentiate the clinical presentation of diabetic kidney disease.
2. Explain the pathophysiology of diabetic kidney disease.
3. Interpret clinical and lab findings to properly diagnose diabetic kidney disease.
4. Outline the management plan for diabetic kidney disease.

## Case Connection

WJ is a 49-year-old male with type 2 diabetes who comes to your clinic for follow-up. He has had diabetes for 15 years and does not strictly follow his insulin regimen. It is tough stopping for lunch and giving myself injections when work is busy, so sometimes I skip a dose or two. But I am not worried. I check my sugar in the morning and it is always 200. His examination is within normal limits except for a blood pressure of 142/94 mm Hg. His hemoglobin A1c is always elevated, and his urine albumin/creatinine ratio is also elevated on the yearly urine study.

How might WJ's poorly controlled diabetes affect his kidney? What can you explain to WJ the importance of controlling his blood sugar? Consider your answers as you read, and we will revisit WJ at the end of the brick.

## What Is Diabetic Kidney Disease (LO #1)?

Diabetic kidney disease (DKD) is a microvascular complication of diabetes. It manifests as a progressive, chronic kidney disease seen in patients with both type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus (DM) and is by far the most common reason patients in the United States start dialysis or receive a kidney transplant. Its features include the appearance of abnormal amounts of albumin in the urine (albuminuria), a decline in the glomerular filtration rate (GFR), or both. It often presents as asymptomatic proteinuria, which can worsen until it reaches nephrotic range or the nephrotic syndrome (heavy proteinuria with edema, hyperalbuminemia, and hyperlipidemia). This progression of proteinuria is seen in the glomerular disease called diabetic nephropathy, but DKD can actually be characterized by several different renal lesions, including disease in the glomeruli, blood vessels, interstitium, or all three.

## Epidemiology

Approximately 25% to 50% of patients with DM will develop diabetic kidney disease. Since about 10% of the US population has diabetes mellitus, that means that 2% to 5% of the entire population (up to 10 million people) may have some level of diabetic kidney disease. That makes it the most common cause of chronic kidney disease by far. Diabetes mellitus can affect all age groups, and so can diabetic kidney disease.

Patients with DKD comprise the largest group of patients with end-stage kidney disease, defined as loss of GFR necessitating renal replacement therapy like dialysis or transplantation. In the United States, more than 280,000 patients with diabetic kidney disease require such treatment each year, incurring an annual cost of more than 20 billion USD. DKD is a big deal!

## How Do Patients With Diabetic Kidney Disease Present (LO #1)?

Diabetic kidney disease is a progressive disease often resulting from poor control of diabetes. As a result, patients may initially be asymptomatic for years. Many patients have their renal disease diagnosed by routine urine or blood testing as part of diabetic care.

However, as the disease progresses, patients may develop increasing edema and uremic symptoms (failed kidneys) like itching, confusion, fatigue, and anorexia—an indication that they need dialysis or transplantation. The chance of these worse complications increases if care is irregular or if glucose control is poor, but they can occur even with optimal management of diabetes.

The kidney function, as indicated by the serum creatinine concentration, or estimated GFR (eGFR), is variable. Patients can present early with decreased GFR, but more often, GFR is normal until later stages of the disease.

DKD occurs in both type 1 and type 2 diabetes, but onset timelines differ due to the difference in presentation of the two forms. In type 1 diabetes, renal disease typically develops after at least 5 years of known disease. The date of onset is typically established with precision as type 1 presents abruptly with an episode of ketoacidosis. In contrast, renal disease may be present much earlier in type 2 diabetes because hyperglycemia often goes unrecognized for years before diagnosis.

## What Is the Pathophysiology of Diabetic Kidney Disease (LO #2)?

Just as diabetes mellitus is a complex metabolic syndrome with many complications and presentations, diabetic kidney disease (DKD) can also come in multiple varieties.

### Risk Factors

Besides having diabetes mellitus, modifiable risk factors for DKD include higher glucose levels (or hemoglobin A1C levels), hypertension, low socioeconomic status (probably reflecting less medical care), and obesity.

Nonmodifiable risk factors include advancing age (perhaps reflecting more years of having diabetes), sex, and family history.

### Pathogenesis

The mechanism of DKD is complex and is a mix of altered cellular biochemistry, increased glomerular pressures, inflammation, and fibrosis. Because these mechanisms contribute differently across patients, some present with isolated proteinuria while others develop progressive renal failure.

**Hyperfiltration and High Glomerular Pressure.** Early in DKD, activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system leads to preferential constriction of the efferent arteriole. This increases pressure within the glomerular capillaries, resulting in increased renal blood flow and an elevated GFR, termed hyperfiltration. Although this increase in GFR may appear beneficial, it is driven by chronically elevated intraglomerular pressure rather than improved filtration efficiency. Sustained high pressure places mechanical stress on the glomerular capillaries, mesangium, and podocytes, promoting mesangial expansion, protein leakage, and progressive glomerular injury. Over time, this leads to fibrosis and a decline in GFR. The glomerulus also hypertrophies, giving rise to the large kidneys seen in many patients with early diabetes.

**Clinical Correlation.** One reason angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors are so useful in diabetic kidney disease is that they reduce this glomerular hyperfiltration and elevated GFR, reducing the fibrosis and glomerular hypertrophy.

**Nonenzymatic Glycation.** Chronic hyperglycemia in diabetes promotes nonenzymatic glycation, a process in which glucose spontaneously binds to proteins without enzymatic regulation. Long-lived proteins in blood vessels and the extracellular matrix are particularly susceptible to this modification. Because this process depends only on glucose concentration, sustained hyperglycemia leads to broad damage by mass action. Glycated proteins develop abnormal structure and function, leading to tissue stiffening and dysfunction. In the kidney, nonenzymatic glycation of glomerular components contributes to thickening of the filtration barrier and progressive loss of filtration capacity.

**Clinical Correlation.** Hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) is used to diagnose diabetes and assess long-term glycemic control. HbA1c reflects the extent of nonenzymatic glycation of hemoglobin, which increases in proportion to average blood glucose levels. Because glycation is driven by mass action, sustained hyperglycemia leads to higher HbA1c values and correlates with increased risk of diabetic complications in other tissues, including the kidney. Red blood cells express insulin-independent GLUT-1 transporters and have a lifespan of approximately 120 days, allowing HbA1c to represent mean blood glucose over the preceding 3–4 months, in contrast to point-of-care glucose measurements that reflect only a single time point.

**Advanced Glycation End Products (AGEs).** NEG leads to formation of advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and occurs when circulating fats or proteins interact with high glucose levels in the diabetic environment. The AGEs may bind to specific receptors on multiple renal cells (RAGE = receptors for AGEs), leading to cytokine secretion and increased inflammation (Figure 1).

For example, increased levels of the cytokine TGF-β cause proliferation of the glomerular mesangial cells and expansion of the mesangial matrix. This gives rise to the nodular appearance of glomeruli in some patients with diabetic kidney disease. The increased cytokines also lead to hyperproliferation of blood vessels. This inflammatory process also damages renal tubules and leads to influx of macrophages. Fibroblasts lay down abnormal collagen and fibronectin, leading to renal scarring. This correlates well with the fall in GFR that occurs in most patients with diabetic kidney disease. In addition, during this inflammatory process, proteinaceous material is deposited within the walls of the arterioles, which thickens the walls, reduces the capillary lumen size, and disrupts blood flow (Figure 2). This is essentially the hyaline arteriolosclerosis characteristic of DKD. This combined hypertensive, inflammatory, and proliferative process eventually leads to glomerular sclerosis, proteinuria (due to a damaged GBM), and declining GFR. These are hallmarks of most cases of advanced DKD.

## Histopathology

We rarely perform renal biopsies in DKD because the clinical presentation is usually characteristic and management does not change based on histology findings; treatment focuses on glycemic and blood pressure control, ACE inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers. This means the proposed sequence of renal lesions in DKD cannot be consistently correlated with histologic stages in individual patients. Consequently, there is substantial interpatient variability in the timing and pattern of kidney injury. Some patients may initially present with declining GFR despite minimal or absent proteinuria. For this reason, DKD is best understood as a clinical syndrome rather than a single, uniform disease process.

That said, histologically, the progression of DKD often follows a predictable pattern of change, at least in a subset of patients often associated with pathology textbooks:

1. Glomerular basement membrane thickens (Figure 5).
2. Glomerular mesangial cells proliferate and lay down more matrix proteins, so the surrounding mesangium begins to expand.
3. This eventually leads to the formation of nodules known as Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules (Figure 3, arrows).

**Clinical Correlation.** Kimmelstiel-Wilson (KW) nodules are spherical, pink (eosinophilic) deposits of hyaline material formed by the excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix (ECM) in the glomerulus. They are composed primarily of basement membrane-like materials, including types I, III, IV, V, and VI collagen, along with laminin, fibronectin, and heparan sulfate proteoglycan.

By immunofluorescence microscopy, there may be linear accentuation of GBMs, typically strongest for immunoglobulin G (IgG) (Figure 4). Absence of accompanying significant GBM C3 staining or crescents, and the clinical history, also aid in differentiating this staining from anti-GBM antibody–mediated glomerulonephritis.

Just be aware that not all patients have these glomerular findings, and some may instead have mostly significant tubular and interstitial inflammation and scarring with mostly normal glomeruli. Because none of these changes impact treatment, the renal biopsy is not currently an important part of management of DKD. Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules are seen in advanced diabetic kidney disease.

## How Do We Diagnose Diabetic Kidney Disease (LO #3)?

If there is regular diabetic care, most patients will have diabetic kidney disease detected first on routine lab screening.

### Albuminuria

In most patients with DKD, the first sign of disease is the presence of consistent increased urine albumin excretion of more than 30 mg albumin per 24 hours. The albuminuria is detected using either a 24-hour urine collection (which is cumbersome) or a spot specimen that measures urine albumin and creatinine concentrations (the albumin/creatinine ratio, or ACR). The ratio of these approximates the result of the 24-hour collection, so it is now the test done most often.

**Clinical Correlation.** To calculate the ACR, divide the spot urine albumin concentration (mg/dL) by the urine creatinine concentration (g/dL). Abnormal albuminuria is more than 30 mg/24h (or above 30 mg/g creatinine on the 24-hour collection).

The albumin/creatinine ratio should be checked yearly in all patients with diabetes to screen for early DKD.

**Clinical Correlation.** Note that the urine dipstick is not a substitute for these tests; it is too insensitive to pick up microalbuminuria.

If DKD has progressed, the ACR can exceed 300 mg/g and may continue to increase to levels greater than 3000 mg/g (nephrotic range proteinuria). It is critical to diagnose DKD in the earliest stage because it can often be reversed at this point by using ACE inhibitors and improving glucose control. An ACR of more than 300 mg/g carries a far worse prognosis.

### Glomerular Filtration Rate

Some patients with DKD develop a decreased GFR, even before they develop abnormal albuminuria. The serum creatinine should be measured during the evaluation and then used to calculate the estimated GFR (eGFR). An eGFR of less than 60 mL/min/1.73 m^2 for greater than 3 months is abnormal, like in any other diabetes mellitus without other evident causes of chronic kidney disease (CKD), most likely means DKD regardless of the level of urine albumin. Note that this is different from traditional teaching, which said that the eGFR did not fall in this condition until after proteinuria increased. We now know that patients with biopsy-proven DKD can have normal urine albumin excretion.

### Other Tests

Because patients with DKD are at risk for hyperkalemia and type 4 renal tubular acidosis, serum electrolytes should be drawn regularly. Important routine diabetic care includes regular measurement of fasting blood glucose and hemoglobin A1c levels, as well as serum lipid panels.

### Renal Biopsy

Unlike most glomerular diseases, renal biopsy is not needed to establish the diagnosis because the clinical pattern is classic. Since biopsies only rarely show other diseases, they are reserved for atypical cases (eg, patients with diabetes who present with sudden edema and heavy proteinuria). A renal biopsy is done only when there are atypical clinical features that make another glomerular disease more likely.

## How Do We Manage Diabetic Kidney Disease (LO #4)?

It is important to slow or prevent the progression of DKD. Glucose control, hypertension control, and the use of an ACE inhibitor can help with this.

### Glucose Control

The central pathology of diabetic kidney disease (DKD) is chronic hyperglycemia; therefore, tight glycemic control is essential to limit nonenzymatic glycation (NEG) and slow disease progression. Because NEG increases with excess circulating glucose, therapy should be individualized to maintain blood glucose within the normal range (70–100 mg/dL). Most patients with diabetes should target an HbA1c <7.0%; detailed diabetes management is covered in the Endocrine course.

**Sodium-Glucose Cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) Inhibitors.** Sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitors block the reabsorption of glucose in the proximal tubule, lowering blood glucose, and inducing osmotic diuresis. Long-lived proteins in blood vessels and the extracellular matrix are particularly susceptible to this modification. Because this process depends only on glucose concentration, sustained hyperglycemia leads to broad damage by mass action.

**Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 (GLP-1) Receptor Agonists.** GLP-1 receptor agonists (eg, semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide) are also recommended in DKD because they provide cardiovascular and renal benefits in addition to glucose lowering. They improve glycemic control by enhancing glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppressing glucagon, slowing gastric emptying, and increasing satiety, often resulting in weight loss. GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce major adverse cardiovascular events and slow the progression of chronic kidney disease.

### Blood Pressure Control

In DKD, the blood pressure target is less than 130 systolic and less than 80 mm Hg diastolic. Hypertension and diabetes act synergistically to accelerate nephropathy because hypertension also causes hyaline arteriolosclerosis. That is why it is especially important to treat hypertension in people with diabetes.

The best choice for antihypertensive medication in DKD is an ACE inhibitor or an ARB. These medications lead to vasodilation of the efferent arteriole, which reduces the extent of hyperfiltration injury, lowers proteinuria, and slows the progression of disease. Care should be taken to avoid hyperkalemia in patients taking these drugs. If this happens, you can add a diuretic to increase potassium excretion or put the patient on a low-potassium diet.

### Non-Steroidal Mineralocorticoid Antagonists

Finerenone is the only available non-steroidal mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (nsMRA) and blocks the mineralocorticoid receptor with greater specificity than mineralocorticoid antagonists like spironolactone. Finerenone reduces aldosterone-driven inflammation and fibrosis in the kidneys and is indicated to reduce CKD progression in those with persistent albuminuria above 30 mg/g despite ACE inhibitors or ARBs and SGLT2 inhibitor use.

### Chronic Kidney Disease Management

As the patient's eGFR drops below 60 mL/min/1.73 m^2, they will gradually fall risk to the usual consequences of advanced chronic kidney disease (eg, hyperphosphatemia, anemia, metabolic bone disease, and hyperkalemia). Many of these complications are worsened in the diabetic environment, so close follow-up is needed. Referral to nephrology consultation should be done when the eGFR falls below 30 mL/min/1.73 m^2 so the patient can be educated about renal replacement therapy and preparation for renal replacement therapy begun.

### Prognosis

Despite optimal treatment, some patients progress to end-stage kidney disease that requires dialysis or kidney transplantation. Prognosis is generally poor at this stage. The average life expectancy once on dialysis is only 5 years for patients with DKD. Death is usually from cardiovascular disease like myocardial infarction. If the patient instead receives a renal transplant, the lifespan is 8 to 18 years, but much of this beneficial effect is explained by healthier patients being referred for transplantation.

## Case Connection (Revisited)

Thinking back to WJ, how do you explain the importance of controlling his blood sugar? WJ has poor diabetic control and diabetic renal disease, based on his elevated urine albumin/creatinine ratio. You explain the test results to WJ. You tell him that poor blood pressure and diabetic control are affecting his kidney function, but with better control, and added medication, further damage can be prevented. You stress the importance of taking medication as prescribed, and you prescribe an ACE inhibitor and a SGLT2 inhibitor. At the 1-month visit, WJ reports that his daily blood sugar measurements are now in normal range. His blood pressure is 118/72 mm Hg.

## Summary

- Diabetic kidney disease (DKD) is a progressive chronic kidney disease due to diabetes mellitus.
- Clinically, patients are often asymptomatic at diagnosis if they are receiving good diabetic care.
- The diagnosis is now typically made by routine lab diagnosis.
- The pathophysiology includes nonenzymatic glycation of proteins, interstitial inflammation, fibrosis, and glomerular hyperfiltration with increased glomerular pressure.
- The diagnosis of DKD is made by detecting urine albumin >30 mg/g on urine albumin/creatinine ratio.
- The serum creatinine concentration should be measured and used to calculate the estimated GFR (eGFR), with <60 mL/min/1.73 m^2 most likely meaning DKD in this disease setting.
- For treatment and prevention, some lifestyle changes should be made by the patient. HbA1c should be less than 7%, blood pressure should be less than 130/80 mm Hg, and an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor or angiotensin receptor-blocker (ARB) as well as an SGLT2 inhibitor should be prescribed.
- Additional therapy to reduce CKD progression includes GLP-1 agonists and nonsteroidal mineralocorticoid antagonists.
- If there is progression to end-stage kidney disease (ESKD), the mean lifespan on dialysis is 5 years, with death usually due to cardiovascular disease.

# Acute Tubular Necrosis

## Learning Objectives

1. Differentiate the clinical presentation of acute tubular necrosis (ATN).
2. Explain the pathophysiology of ATN.
3. Interpret clinical and lab findings to properly diagnose ATN.
4. Outline the management plan for ATN.

## Case Connection

NB, a 56-year-old male, collapses on the golf course. Paramedics arrive within 20 minutes and perform CPR. Initial blood pressure is 60/40 mm Hg. He is transferred to the ED. Blood pressure stabilizes at 72/40 mm Hg. His electrocardiogram (ECG) shows a likely acute anterior myocardial infarction (MI), and he is immediately taken to the catheterization lab for coronary angiography. His admission serum creatinine levels are normal, but 2 days later they have tripled. NB's blood pressure is normal, and his physical exam is unremarkable. What is the likely cause of NB's acute kidney injury (AKI)?

## What Is Acute Tubular Necrosis? (LO #1)

Acute tubular necrosis (ATN) is not a disease, but a pathologic description of the abrupt death of epithelial cells lining the renal tubules. It has many different causes, including **ischemia** and **toxins**.

ATN is a form of **acute kidney injury (AKI)**, which is a persistent loss of kidney function or abnormal kidney function over a short period of time. As such, it leads to a drop in the **glomerular filtration rate (GFR)** and an acute rise in the serum **creatinine** concentration. It can also cause potentially lethal fluid and electrolyte disorders. ATN often resolves completely once the original insult is gone, but it can persist as **chronic kidney disease** in some cases.

ATN can occur in any part of the **tubular system**, the portion of the nephron distal to the glomerular filtration unit. After the blood is filtered by the glomerulus, it flows through the tubules as ultrafiltrate, where key molecules are reabsorbed and secreted by renal tubular epithelial cells. ATN reflects loss of those functioning cells.

ATN can develop markedly diminished urine output (oliguria) and severe electrolyte disorders like **acidosis** or **hyperkalemia** as a result. Other patients may have a sudden failure of kidney function that leads to large and unregulated urine output (polyuria) and **hypokalemia**.

## Epidemiology

ATN is the most common cause of AKI and the most common cause of AKI among hospitalized patients. There are approximately 18 cases of ATN per 100,000 individuals worldwide. Most patients are hospitalized and in an intensive care unit setting, and so they have a high mortality rate.

## How Do Patients With Acute Tubular Necrosis Present? (LO #1)

Patients with ATN are usually sick and often hospitalized because of a severe, acute underlying illness. This is because the main causes of ATN are either very low blood perfusion (e.g., shock, severe trauma, and hemorrhage) or toxic injuries to the tubules from things like myoglobin (released from muscle cells during crush injuries) or drugs like aminoglycoside antibiotics (used in severe Gram-negative infections), radiology contrast dyes, or chemotherapy drugs.

Specific patient symptoms vary from none to those of underlying shock and sepsis (e.g., dizziness, fever, coma). Symptoms caused by the renal injury itself may be hard to discern, noticing that in a hospitalized patient the renal failure may be the first sign. ATN is most often diagnosed not by patient reports but by physician observations: a rising creatinine over time in a hospitalized patient. Once urine output is falling, fluid overload (with maintained urine volume) depending on the type and degree of insult to the kidney develop.

## What Is the Pathophysiology of Acute Tubular Necrosis? (LO #2)

ATN is a form of **intrinsic (intrarenal) AKI**, meaning a decline in the kidney function (i.e., lower GFR) arising inside the kidney. This contrasts with **prerenal AKI**, in which the problem is distal to the kidney (impaired renal drainage).

These categories sometimes intertwine: prerenal AKI causes a transient drop in renal function as a result of reduced perfusion of the kidney and is easily reversed with restoration of perfusion (e.g., transfusion or administration of saline). However, if the reduced renal perfusion is prolonged or very severe, the prerenal AKI cannot be reversed by simply restoring renal blood flow. At this point, restoration of renal perfusion cannot reverse the AKI and the problem becomes a form of intrinsic AKI.

ATN occurs when the kidneys are injured either by hypoxic injury (reduced perfusion) or by toxins that damage the tubular cells. We will now go into the specific causes of each.

### Causes

- **Ischemia-related ATN**: Typically results from reduced renal blood flow, such as in sepsis and shock (systemic hypotension) or severe heart failure (decreased blood flow). Ischemia leads to reduced oxygen delivery to the tubules. With prolonged tissue hypoxia, the tubular cells become injured, leading to the term "acute tubular necrosis."
- **Nephrotoxin-related ATN**: Primarily results from circulating molecules (endogenous and exogenous) that directly damage tubular cells. Among the endogenous molecules that can cause ATN are myoglobin (released after massive muscle breakdown) and free hemoglobin (released after massive hemolysis). Numerous drugs and exogenous toxins also cause ATN. We will review some below, starting with the most common drug causes of ATN: aminoglycoside antibiotics, radiologic contrast agents, chemotherapy agents, and ethylene glycol.

## Pathophysiology of Ischemic Acute Tubular Necrosis

The blood supply of the kidneys occurs via end-arteries, without collateral circulation. Therefore, the renal circulation is very vulnerable to upstream reduction of blood flow. In addition, the kidneys have a high metabolic rate and high oxygen consumption, so not only is the oxygen supply low, but the demand is high. In ischemic ATN, the combined high oxygen consumption and reduced delivery lead to tissue hypoxia and death of the renal tubular epithelial cells.

ATN results from a lack of reabsorption, damage caused by hypoxic injury (reduced perfusion) or by toxins that damage the tubular cells. He will also have damage to the specific causes of each.

## Pathophysiology of Nephrotoxic Acute Tubular Necrosis

Nephrotoxic-related ATN primarily results from circulating molecules (endogenous and exogenous) that directly damage tubular cells. What are two possible reasons why nephrotoxic agents might cause ATN? First, they may directly cause necrosis as the tubular cells are exposed to the circulating agent. Second, some drugs (e.g., contrast agents) cause prolonged vasoconstriction of the renal vessels (like the ischemic causes described above).

### Myoglobin and Hemoglobin

In **rhabdomyolysis**, myoglobin released from necrosed muscle cells is filtered into the tubules and is toxic to them. This happens after muscle trauma (e.g., crush injuries after accidents), electrolyte disorders like severe hypokalemia or hypophosphatemia, heatstroke, sepsis, statin drugs, snakebites, viral infections, and carbon monoxide toxicity. Hints for a diagnosis are a urine dipstick result that is positive for heme but no RBCs are seen on urine microscopy.

**Clinical correlation**: Urine dipsticks detect both hemoglobin and myoglobin and do not distinguish between them.
- Positive urine dipstick, with RBCs present on microscopy = hemoglobin.
- Positive urine dipstick, with no RBCs present on microscopy = myoglobin.

Filtered free hemoglobin is toxic to the tubules. High free blood hemoglobin occurs after massive hemolysis (e.g., ABO blood type mismatch).

### Aminoglycosides

These antibiotics are freely filtered by the glomerulus and taken up by the proximal tubular epithelial cells. They have a long half-life there because they alter mitochondrial metabolism, leading to cell death. Unlike most forms of ATN, aminoglycosides usually cause a non-oliguric form of ATN, in which the patient continues to have a near-normal urine output. To prevent ATN, **tobramycin** seems to cause ATN less than do the other drugs in this class.

### Amphotericin B

This antifungal agent amphotericin B creates pores in cell membranes by binding to sterols to increase membrane permeability and disrupt electrolyte gradients. As with aminoglycosides, ATN occurs with sustained use with amphotericin B, at least 5 to 7 days.

### Cisplatin

The chemotherapy drug cisplatin primarily affects the proximal tubules; its hydrolyzed metabolite binds DNA, RNA, proteins, and phospholipids to kill cells.

### Radiocontrast

We don't completely understand how intravenous radiocontrast agents cause toxicity, and some new data suggest that the AKI seen in some patients who receive contrast represents a continuation of the patient's original disease and not the contrast effects. In any case, the syndrome is unique because it tends to resolve quickly, often after only 48 hours. Tubular necrosis here is usually much more limited and milder than with the other nephrotoxins. Current theories include a combination of direct cytotoxic effects and stimulation of renal vasoconstriction. In many patients, the latter effect (causing prerenal AKI, not ATN) is all that is seen.

Risk factors include volume depletion, diabetes, or heart failure, so this syndrome is now named **contrast-associated AKI**.

**Clinical correlation**: Besides being nephrotoxic agents, aminoglycosides and cisplatin are also two of the most common ototoxic drugs.

### Other Drugs

Among the other drugs that cause ATN are the antibiotics vancomycin and pentamidine, the antivirals foscarnet and cidofovir, and chemotherapy mechanistic target of rapamycin (mTOR) inhibitors such as everolimus.

### Ingested Toxins

**Ethylene glycol** (anti-freeze) ingestion leads to toxic metabolites that cause ATN. The ATN is primarily a result of glycolate-induced damage to tubules, although tubule obstruction from precipitated oxalate crystals may contribute. Ethylene glycol will also cause increased serum osmolality and an anion-gap metabolic acidosis from the formation of oxalic acid.

## Course of Tubular Injury

**Inciting Event**. After one of these events described above, the damaged cells lose their ability to reabsorb and secrete but are still intact. This leads to their inability to reabsorb sodium, which results in high sodium levels in the tubular fluid and urine. Clinically, the only indication of renal involvement may be a modest decline in urine output and a rise in BUN.

**Maintenance Phase**. In this phase of ATN, the cells necrose and slough into the renal tubule, leading to the tubular epithelial cells and casts seen on urinalysis. This phase is characterized by sustained decreases in urine output to between 40 and 400 mL per day (oliguria), salt and water overload, rising BUN concentrations, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, and other manifestations of uremia.

Why are urine output and GFR usually low in the maintenance phase?
- The necrosed tubular cells slough into the tubule and block blood flow.
- The necrosis of tubules causes filtered water and electrolytes to back-leak through the disrupted epithelial layers into the kidney's stroma.
- Low sodium reabsorption causes increased sodium delivery to the macula densa in the distal nephron. In turn, this causes reflex constriction of the afferent arteriole through **tubuloglomerular feedback (TGF)**. The afferent arteriolar constriction lowers GFR.

**Recovery Phase**. During this phase, the tubules reestablish their structure and bulk stop, so urine volume increases. The cells gradually recover their resorptive and secretory abilities. Because so much urea, sodium, and other osmoles as well as water have built up in the body, they now pour out into the urine. This **diuretic phase** sometimes leads to large urine outputs, and the patient must be given fluids to avoid hypotension.

ATN often resolves spontaneously. Remember, however, that it can also be fatal, especially during the oliguric phase when urine output is low.

## Histopathology

The histology of ATN is characterized by:
- Flattening of the normal plump, cuboidal cells (attenuation).
- Loss of the **brush border** (the normal "shaggy" internal edge of the tubules is lost and instead looks smooth).
- Necrotic cells slough off into the center of the tubules.

## Complications

Because of the drop in GFR and urine output, metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, and volume overload are all common and can result in death. Patients who have maintained urine output have lower mortality.

**Clinical correlation**: Signs of hyperkalemia on ECG include peaked T waves and ventricular arrhythmias, which may be fatal.

## How Do We Diagnose Acute Tubular Necrosis? (LO #3)

ATN is usually a laboratory diagnosis detected when urine output falls or serum creatinine rises. It should be watched for in any patient with sepsis or hypotension, or who is exposed to one of the nephrotoxins.

### Physical Examination

Physical examination findings initially will point to the cause of the ATN, for example, fever and hypotension in sepsis. Once the GFR and urine output fall, the patient may develop signs of volume overload (e.g., peripheral and pulmonary edema) or uremia (e.g., nausea, fatigue).

### Serum Studies

Patients with ATN will typically be diagnosed because they have a low GFR, seen as a rising serum creatinine concentration in the days following the renal insult. These patients must be closely monitored for falling serum **bicarbonate** (metabolic acidosis) or rising serum **potassium** levels.

### Urine Studies

Typical urine studies include urinalysis and **fractional excretion of sodium (FENa)**.

**Urinalysis**: In ATN, renal tubular epithelial cells die and slough into the tubule. These can be seen as single cells or as casts. As the tubular cells die and lyse, debris from necrosis accumulates and obstructs the tubules, resulting in "muddy-brown" granular casts.

**Fractional Excretion of Sodium**: The FENa shows how well the kidney is reabsorbing sodium. A low percentage means that sodium is significantly reabsorbed. The typical value for FENa ranges from 0.5% to 5%, depending on sodium intake. This low percentage of excreted sodium tells us that sodium is mostly reabsorbed by the kidney.

FENa is calculated from two urine concentrations and two serum concentrations:

FENa = (Urine sodium x Serum creatinine) / (Serum sodium x Urine creatinine) x 100%

Because the tubules are damaged in ATN, the FENa rises because there is poor sodium reabsorption. FENa comparison in ATN vs prerenal AKI (postrenal AKI shows variable FENa):
- In ATN, there is increased urine sodium concentration and increased FENa (>2%) as a result of the loss of tubular reabsorption of ions, including sodium.
- In prerenal AKI (volume depletion), there is activation of the **renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system** causing the kidneys to maximize salt and water reabsorption to conserve volume. Therefore, there is minimal urinary sodium and a low FENa of <1%.

The typical urinalysis findings in patients with ATN are muddy-brown granular casts, tubular epithelial cells, and tubular cell casts.

## How Do We Manage Acute Tubular Necrosis? (LO #4)

ATN cannot be cured, but instead, the patient with ATN is supported until their tubules regenerate on their own, which can take several days. The aim is to keep them alive long enough so that this regeneration can occur. The most important interventions involve treatment of ATN: typically includes discontinuing potentially nephrotoxic agents, supporting blood pressure, and treating electrolyte disorders like hyperkalemia.

Patients should be assessed for volume depletion, and any deficits should be promptly replenished with normal saline. Patients with oliguria with volume overload (like pulmonary edema) may be given loop diuretics (e.g., furosemide) to improve urine output during the maintenance phase. However, there is little evidence that this "converts" oliguric to non-oliguric ATN, which is easier to manage and carries less risk to the patient. Normal urine output returns on its own when the tubules heal and regenerate. This is even considered a marker of recovering tubule function.

Despite supportive treatment, ATN may persist, resulting in worsening kidney function and decreasing GFR. These patients may experience uremic symptoms (confusion, pericarditis) caused by the accumulation of nitrogenous wastes in the blood, severe electrolyte disorders, or metabolic acidosis. In these situations, acute hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis may be necessary. As kidney function recovers, dialysis may be discontinued.

## Case Connection

Thinking back to NB, what is the likely cause of his AKI? NB experienced a prolonged period of hypotension. The most likely cause of his AKI is ATN secondary to ischemic injury to the kidney during the time his blood pressure was so low. In addition, there may have been a second hit to the kidney from the contrast media NB received during his angiography.

Ischemic death of renal tubular epithelial cells impairs the absorption of sodium and water. The test results support this, as NB's urine sodium level is high, and the FENa is high. The treatment ordered for NB's ATN is mainly supportive.

## Summary

- Acute tubular necrosis (ATN) is a form of acute kidney injury (AKI) that results from the death of renal tubular epithelial cells, usually caused by ischemic or nephrotoxic injury.
- ATN is one of the most common causes of AKI and is responsible for nearly half of all AKI among hospitalized patients.
- Patients usually receive a diagnosis in the hospital, not by patient complaints but by physician observations, for example, when their urine output falls and serum creatinine levels rise.
- Ischemic ATN results from reduced blood flow, as in shock, sepsis, or severe heart failure. Nephrotoxic causes of ATN include aminoglycosides, amphotericin B, cisplatin, ingested toxins, myoglobin (rhabdomyolysis), and hemolysis.
- Complications can include acidosis, hyperkalemia, and volume overload.
- Serum studies show rising creatinine levels. Urinalysis usually shows muddy-brown granular casts. ATN usually has a fractional excretion of sodium (FENa) >2%, distinguishing it from prerenal AKI.
- Management of ATN focuses on removing the renal insult (e.g., hypotension, drugs) and is then supportive. Prolonged ATN may lead to the need for dialysis.

# Acute Interstitial Nephritis

## Learning Objectives

1. Differentiate the clinical presentation of a patient with acute interstitial nephritis (AIN).
2. Explain the pathophysiology of acute interstitial nephritis.
3. Interpret clinical and lab findings to properly diagnose acute interstitial nephritis.
4. Outline the management plan for acute interstitial nephritis.

## Case Connection

JM is a 53-year-old male with seasonal allergies who comes to the clinic with back pain, a rash, and reports of feeling feverish. He had a recent cold and was treated with antibiotics. Lab work shows an elevated creatinine.

## What Is Acute Interstitial Nephritis? (LO #1)

Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) is an inflammatory reaction in the kidney that causes acute kidney injury (AKI), a sudden decline in renal function. AIN is a single disease but is actually a family of kidney diseases characterized by renal inflammation (esp. of the interstitium, the space that surrounds the renal tubules).

AIN is most often caused by a reaction to a drug, but it occasionally can be caused by infection or autoimmune disease. AIN with drugs like antibiotics, NSAIDs, and proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) often happens to stimulate an allergic reaction; this causes further inflammation of the interstitium, and we end up seeing a rising serum creatinine level.

## Epidemiology

Of all patients undergoing renal biopsy for AKI (note that biopsy is not common in AKI), AIN can be seen in 1 out of 3 cases. Most cases of AIN (>95%) occur because of:

- infection, or
- allergic drug reaction.

Prevalence is higher in older adults and in patients taking NSAIDs and proton pump inhibitors (PPIs). However, the true incidence of AIN is likely underreported because most patients with AKI do not undergo renal biopsy.

## How Do Patients With Acute Interstitial Nephritis Present? (LO #1)

Patients with AIN are most often **asymptomatic**, and the disease is suspected when lab test results show a decline in renal function (e.g., a rise in the serum creatinine levels).

Patients with allergic reactions to a drug can present with fever, rash, and hematuria. The classical triad described is:

- fever,
- rash,
- and eosinophilia,

but this full triad is seen in less than 10% of patients. The rise in creatinine levels (i.e., drop in glomerular filtration rate [GFR]) typically occurs 7 to 10 days after starting the drug.

## What Is the Pathophysiology of Acute Interstitial Nephritis? (LO #2)

AIN is a state of **renal interstitial inflammation** that sometimes also affects the tubules. The pathophysiology underlying this disorder can be classified broadly into two different categories, which we will now discuss.

### Pathogenesis

AIN is driven by infiltration of inflammatory cells into the renal interstitium. This leads to a release of local antigens that stimulate an immune response. This causes further inflammatory cell infiltration and activation of proinflammatory and chemoattractant cytokines. The result of this damage to renal tubular cells is tubular dysfunction.

The inciting event for this inflammatory reaction can be allergic or nonallergic:

- **Allergic.** With an allergic inflammatory reaction, a combined type I (immunoglobulin E [IgE]) and type IV (T cells) hypersensitivity reaction to medications occurs, such as to penicillin. In these cases, the medications act as haptens (small molecules that attach to larger carriers to elicit an immune response) that ultimately bind to carrier proteins to induce the hypersensitivity reaction.
- **Nonallergic.** In a nonallergic inflammatory reaction, infection or autoimmune diseases directly stimulate an immune response to the kidney with renal interstitial cells as bystanders of the nonspecific immune response.

The mechanism of the most common form of AIN caused by NSAIDs is not well understood.

## Causes

As noted, AIN most often occurs after the administration of medications that act like haptens. Less commonly, AIN may be caused by systemic diseases that may provoke the inflammatory response. Let's review these two causes next.

**Medications.** Many drugs are reported to cause AIN. Some of the more common ones are:

- NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen or naproxen) (most common cause overall)
- Mesalamine (5-Aminosalicylic acid) (an NSAID used for inflammatory bowel disease)
- Antibiotics (eg, penicillin, cephalosporins, rifampin)
- Thiazide and loop diuretics
- Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) for gastritis and peptic ulcer disease
- Programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1) checkpoint inhibitor chemotherapy drugs (e.g., nivolumab)

**Inflammatory Diseases.** Systemic diseases that cause AIN include:

- Autoimmune diseases (e.g., systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjogren syndrome, sarcoidosis)
- Chronic urinary tract obstruction
- Infections (eg, acute bacterial pyelonephritis)

## Histopathology

Renal biopsy is not done very often for AKI, so we do not often review the histology of AIN. Biopsies are more likely to be performed if the course is protracted or if the temporal relationship to a drug is not clear.

However, if done, histology provides a view of the inflammatory process in AIN. The biopsy specimen in Figure 1 shows inflammatory cell infiltrates, including eosinophils, and tubular injury. The glomeruli and blood vessels are typically normal. However, biopsy material is not always available.

## How Do We Diagnose Acute Interstitial Nephritis? (LO #3)

Most patients have no symptoms and AIN is diagnosed on routine lab studies. A presumptive diagnosis can be made based on the clinical picture and response to therapy, but a definitive diagnosis requires confirmation with a kidney biopsy; however, a kidney biopsy is not performed very often, because withdrawing the offending drug usually solves the problem.

### Serum Studies

AIN is a form of AKI, leading to a decline in GFR. Consequently, in serum studies, we will see a rise in the serum urea nitrogen and serum creatinine levels.

Some patients with allergic variants (eg, those caused by antibiotics) may have high blood eosinophil counts, which can help with diagnosis in uncertain cases.

### Urine Studies

Urinalysis with microscopy is the best diagnostic test for AIN. Note that the fractional excretion of sodium (FENa), which is useful in diagnosing acute tubular necrosis (ATN), is not useful in suspected AIN.

**Urinalysis.** Patients with AIN may show white blood cells (WBCs) and WBC casts (Figures 2a and 2b).

At first, this may look like pyelonephritis, but what's missing? There will be no bacteria because there is no infection. Sometimes we can see urine eosinophils, which can be detected using Hansel or Wright stains, but this finding is not reliable for diagnosis. With ongoing inflammation, microscopic hematuria may also be noted.

### Clinical Correlation

On urinalysis, the presence of **white blood cell (WBC) casts** serves as a pathognomonic marker for inflammation of the renal parenchyma, helping to localize the pathology to the kidney rather than the lower urinary tract. While these casts are frequently associated with pyelonephritis, in the clinical context of peripheral eosinophilia and a recent drug exposure, they strongly point toward a diagnosis of AIN.

If proteinuria is present, the urine protein concentration is usually low, that is, 1+ or 2+ on a dipstick test or <1 g on a 24-hour collection. An exception to this is NSAID-induced AIN, where proteinuria is much more common, and patients can have nephrotic-range proteinuria (>3.5 g in 24 hours). The reasons for this are unknown.

## Differential Diagnosis

The other diagnoses sometimes confused with AIN include conditions that lead to intrarenal types of AKI. These include ATN, renal papillary necrosis, and acute glomerulonephritis (Table 1).

### Table 1 Comparison of different causes of AKI

| Cause | Urinalysis findings | Proteinuria |
|---|---|---|
| ATN | Muddy-brown granular casts, renal tubular epithelial cells | Uncommon |
| AIN | WBCs (+/- eosinophils) and WBC casts | <1 g in 24 h; often >3 g in 24 h when caused by NSAIDs |
| Renal papillary necrosis | RBCs, tubular epithelial cells, may see WBCs, bacteriuria | Can show proteinuria |
| Acute glomerulonephritis | Dysmorphic RBCs with RBC casts | >4 g in 24 hours |

## How Do We Manage Acute Interstitial Nephritis? (LO #4)

There is no specific treatment for AIN. Similar to many other types of AKI, the primary treatment is supportive. If the etiology of the AIN is an allergic reaction to a toxic drug, the key is to quickly remove the offending agent. When patients stop taking the offending medications early in the disease, it typically results in complete recovery. Other nephrotoxins like contrast agents or other nephrotoxic drugs are to be avoided.

### Clinical Correlation

Glucocorticoids (e.g., prednisone, methylprednisone, dexamethasone) can be used in severe allergic AIN, but if used, they need to be employed quickly to have an effect. Glucocorticoids are not useful in NSAID-induced AIN.

### Clinical Correlation

Uncommonly, patients with features of renal failure may require renal replacement therapy, with correction of electrolyte disorders and possibly dialysis.

With an allergic reaction to a toxic drug, the prognosis is excellent for a full recovery of renal function if the diagnosis is made early and the offending drug is withdrawn.

## Case Conclusion

JM is told the condition he has is called acute interstitial nephritis, and it is frequently caused by an allergic reaction to antibiotics. The treatment is as simple as stopping the drug. At follow-up 3 days later, JM reports that he feels 100% better. His serum creatinine levels are improving. Most colds are viral, so he likely did not require the antibiotics that caused the problem.

## Summary

- Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN) is an inflammatory reaction in the kidney that causes acute kidney injury (AKI).
- Most cases (>95%) of AIN occur as a result of an infection or allergic drug reaction.
- Patients are often asymptomatic; disease is suspected by a decline in renal function as shown on lab testing results.
- Patients with an allergic reaction can present with rash, fever, and hematuria.
- AIN is typically caused by a hypersensitivity allergic reaction to medications like nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), penicillins, and proton pump inhibitors that act as haptens to stimulate an immune response.
- AIN is also caused by infection or autoimmune disease that directly stimulates an immune response to the kidney.
- Histology shows interstitial edema, infiltration of eosinophils, and tubular injury in AIN.
- There will be a rise in serum urea nitrogen and serum creatinine levels and the presence of urine WBCs and WBC casts; sometimes there are urine eosinophils.
- Proteinuria may be present.
- Primary management is supportive; make sure to discontinue any offending medications.
- With an allergic reaction to a toxic drug, the prognosis is excellent for full renal function recovery if diagnosed early.

transcript:
# Renal Papillary Necrosis

## Overview

Renal papillary necrosis (RPN) is a clinical syndrome marked by ischemic and inflammatory destruction of the renal papillae — the medullary tips of the collecting ducts that drain urine into the renal pelvis. The damaged papillae can slough off, causing ureteral obstruction, gross hematuria, and acute or chronic kidney injury. RPN is most common in older adult patients, but can occur at any age. In sickle cell disease (SCD) it is common and may present at very young ages; classic analgesic nephropathy typically appears after age 40.

## Learning Objectives

1. Differentiate the clinical presentation of renal papillary necrosis.
2. Explain the pathophysiology of renal papillary necrosis.
3. Interpret clinical and lab findings to properly diagnose renal papillary necrosis.
4. Outline the management plan for renal papillary necrosis.

## Clinical Presentation

- Most patients with RPN are **asymptomatic**; the diagnosis is often detected incidentally on imaging of the kidneys done for another reason.
- Symptomatic patients typically have one or more of:
  - Flank or back pain
  - Gross or microscopic hematuria (cloudy/bloody urine)
  - Dysuria
  - Pyuria
  - Polyuria (impaired medullary concentrating ability/loop of Henle dysfunction)
  - Renal colic from sloughed papillae causing ureteral outflow obstruction, mimicking nephrolithiasis
- Patients with SCD-associated RPN may show the same signs, plus features of the underlying sickle cell crisis.

## Pathophysiology

### Relevant anatomy

- Collecting ducts merge to form the **renal papillae**, which empty filtrate into the calyces and renal pelvis, then through the ureters to the bladder.
- The renal papillae are perfused by the **vasa recta**, small straight arterioles arising from the efferent arterioles of juxtamedullary nephrons. The vasa recta carry blood that has already been filtered at the glomerulus, so it is concentrated (hyperosmolar) and relatively hypoxic, especially under the influence of ADH.
- This makes the papillae particularly vulnerable to **ischemia, vasospasm, and injury** to the vasa recta. Reduced blood flow leaves the papillae oxygen-poor; toxins or inflammation can directly damage them, leading to **ischemic papillary necrosis**.

### Major causes of RPN (mnemonic: **POSTCARDS**)

- **P**yelonephritis (severe/recurrent)
- **O**bstruction of the urinary tract
- **S**ickle cell hemoglobinopathies (including SCD and sickle trait)
- **T**uberculosis
- **C**irrhosis of the liver
- **A**nalgesic abuse (chronic high-dose NSAIDs/acetaminophen-containing combinations)
- **R**enal transplant rejection
- **D**iabetes mellitus (microvascular disease)
- **S**ystemic vasculitis

### Key mechanisms

- **Sickle Cell Disease:** sickled RBCs in the hypertonic/hypoxic medulla occlude vasa recta, producing ischemia of the papillae.
- **Diabetes Mellitus:** microvascular narrowing of the vasa recta reduces medullary blood flow.
- **Analgesic nephropathy:** chronic high-dose NSAIDs inhibit vasodilatory prostaglandin synthesis, causing vasoconstriction of the vasa recta and ischemic papillary necrosis. Risk is highest with prolonged/high-dose use and/or high-risk physiology (volume depletion, CKD). Classic presentation is “sloughed papillae” producing ureteral obstruction and gross hematuria that mimics nephrolithiasis.
- **Pyelonephritis/obstruction:** inflammation and increased intrapelvic pressure compromise medullary perfusion.

## Histopathology

- RPN is rarely diagnosed by biopsy because the diagnosis is usually clinical.
- Histologically: papillae with necrotic tissue sloughing into the renal pelvis, **tubular necrosis**, and **neutrophilic inflammatory infiltrates**.

## Diagnosis

There are no specific lab tests that directly diagnose RPN — the diagnosis is made by combining history, risk factors, and findings consistent with the clinical setting (e.g., SCD, diabetes mellitus, chronic NSAID use).

### Urine Studies

- Urinalysis: typically shows hematuria and epithelial cells; under microscopy may show RBCs and tubular epithelial cells.
- If pyelonephritis is the underlying cause, bacteria, WBCs, and WBC casts may also be seen.

### Serum Studies

- Acute or subacute rise in serum **creatinine** due to reduced GFR.

### Imaging

- Imaging is not specific for acute RPN. Renal **ultrasound** or **CT** is used mainly to rule out kidney stones, which can produce similar flank pain and hematuria.
- In acute disease, kidneys often look normal on ultrasound. Calcifications may be seen on X-ray or CT but are neither sensitive nor specific.
- In chronic disease (chronic SCD, chronic analgesic use): small, echogenic kidneys with calcified papillae on ultrasound or CT.
- IV contrast is generally avoided due to risk of contrast nephropathy and acute tubular necrosis on top of existing injury.

## Management

- **Treat the underlying cause:**
  - Pyelonephritis: appropriate antibiotics.
  - Obstruction: relieve obstruction.
  - Sickle cell painful crisis: aggressive IV hydration, pain control, and **hydroxyurea** for long-term reduction of sickling and crises.
  - Analgesic nephropathy: stop NSAIDs and other offending agents.
- **General supportive care:**
  - Maintain adequate hydration to prevent further ischemic injury.
  - Avoid further nephrotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, IV contrast, aminoglycosides).
  - Keep blood pressure and glycemia controlled in diabetic patients.
  - Transfuse if symptomatic anemia/hemolysis is present.
- **Long-term outcomes:** patients with chronic SCD or chronic analgesic use may progress to chronic kidney disease and end-stage renal disease requiring dialysis or transplantation.

## Case Connection

A 17-year-old male with SCD presents to the ED with abdominal pain, chills, and blood in his urine after a hard workout the prior day, leaving him volume-depleted. Renal ultrasound shows hydronephrosis and a hyperechoic mass in the kidney (a sloughed papilla blocking flow). Vital signs, urinalysis, and clinical context support a diagnosis of RPN precipitated by a sickle cell painful crisis. He is admitted for IV fluids, pain control, and supportive care, and is discharged after roughly 72 hours with renal function returning to baseline.

## Summary

- RPN is the death of tubular cells in the renal papillae of the kidney’s collecting system.
- Most common causes are sickle cell disease (SCD), diabetes mellitus, and chronic analgesic (NSAID) use; other causes follow the **POSTCARDS** mnemonic.
- Most patients are asymptomatic; some have flank/back pain, dysuria, hematuria, and/or cloudy urine, or renal colic from sloughed papillae causing ureteral obstruction.
- Mechanism: occlusion, vasospasm, or injury to the **vasa recta** decreases blood flow to the papillae, producing ischemia and necrosis.
- Histology: necrotic papillae, tubular necrosis, neutrophilic inflammatory infiltrates.
- Workup: urinalysis, serum creatinine, renal ultrasound or CT (without contrast).
- Management: treat the underlying cause, hydrate, avoid nephrotoxic drugs; supportive care for SCD includes hydroxyurea.

transcript:
# Cystitis and Pyelonephritis

## Overview

Cystitis and pyelonephritis are among the most common medical problems encountered in clinic (outpatient) and hospital (inpatient) settings. Cystitis is an infection of the bladder (lower urinary tract), and pyelonephritis is an infection of the kidneys (part of the upper urinary tract). They commonly occur independently or together. Pyelonephritis is a more serious infection than cystitis.

Cystitis is particularly common in females. They respond easily to therapy and require little workup.

## Clinical Presentation (LO1)

### Cystitis

Patients with cystitis tend to present with:
- Painful urination (dysuria)
- Urinary frequency and urgency
- Suprapubic pain
- Sometimes gross hematuria (visible blood in urine)
- In females, these symptoms occur in the absence of vaginal symptoms
- Fever is not usually present

### Pyelonephritis

Suspect pyelonephritis if the patient has cystitis symptoms plus:
- Fever and chills
- Flank pain
- Costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness
- Nausea and vomiting
- Elderly patients may show confusion or personality changes

Patients with cystitis are usually afebrile; those with pyelonephritis most often develop high-grade fever with chills.

## Pathophysiology (LO2)

Most patients suffer from cystitis because bacteria from the fecal flora colonize the **urinary meatus** (the opening of the urethra where urine exits). These bacteria climb up the urethra into the bladder as they multiply. If these pathogens ascend further and reach the kidneys through the ureters, they cause pyelonephritis (Figure 1).

Pyelonephritis can also occur when bacteria in the blood (bacteremia) or lymphatics seed the kidneys (hematogenous or lymphatic spread). Pyelonephritis tends to spare the glomeruli and blood vessels and remain confined to the renal parenchyma and tubules. If preferentially spared, it can lead to severe sepsis or permanent kidney damage.

### Bacterial Causes of Urinary Tract Infections

- **Escherichia coli**: most common cause of UTIs. The proximity of the urethra to the anus allows for easy access (E. coli causes 75–95% of cystitis and pyelonephritis).
- **Staphylococcus saprophyticus**: a gram-positive coccus. Like E. coli, S. saprophyticus is normal flora of the genitourinary tract and perineum. These bacteria are culprits in sexually active females. Sexual activity can introduce bacteria into the urethra, either from the partner or from bacteria already present on the patient.
- **Proteus mirabilis, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Enterobacter cloacae, Enterococcus faecalis, Serratia marcescens, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa** are associated with UTIs acquired in hospitals or nursing homes and tend to be drug resistant. Proteus mirabilis infection is associated with struvite kidney stones that can obstruct the urinary tract and cause further infections.

## Risk Factors

### Cystitis
- Female sex (shorter urethra, proximity to anus)
- Sexual activity
- Diabetes
- Obesity
- Prostatic hypertrophy or lack of circumcision (in men)
- In children: anatomic anomalies like horseshoe kidney, ureteral duplication, posterior urethral valves, or vesicoureteral reflux, often leading to hydronephrosis or obstruction

### Pyelonephritis
- Diabetes
- HIV/AIDS
- Advanced age
- Catheters, stents, nephrostomy tubes, or urinary diversions
- Congenital or acquired anatomic/urodynamic abnormalities (e.g., stones)

## Diagnosis (LO3)

### Uncomplicated vs Complicated UTIs

- **Uncomplicated cystitis**: occurs in females who are not pregnant, have normal immune systems, and have no urologic abnormalities (stones, tumors, structural abnormalities).
- **Complicated cystitis**: more serious. Includes UTIs in all males, females with recurrent UTIs (2+ infections in 6 months or 3+ in 1 year), urologic abnormalities, indwelling catheters, diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, immunosuppression (HIV, organ transplant, cytotoxic drugs), and pregnancy.

### Diagnosis of Uncomplicated Cystitis

Diagnosis can be made by **history and physical exam alone**. Laboratory tests, including urinalysis and urine culture, are **not needed** for the diagnosis.

### Differential Diagnosis of Cystitis

Not all cystitis is infectious:
- **Hemorrhagic cystitis**: bladder inflammation with bleeding caused by radiation, chemotherapy (e.g., cyclophosphamide, ifosfamide), viruses, or parasites.
- **Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome**: chronic bladder pain without infection or inflammation, possibly linked to cytokine-mediated hypersensitivity. Pain worsens as the bladder fills and improves with voiding. It often coexists with fibromyalgia or irritable bowel syndrome.

### Diagnosis of Complicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis

Clinical diagnosis is based on symptoms associated with the list of complicating factors above (e.g., diabetes, pregnancy). In pyelonephritis, fever, chills, and costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness can be assessed by tapping the area of the back overlying the kidneys. If pyelonephritis is present, this maneuver will elicit pain.

If the evaluation reveals signs of urinary tract obstruction, stones, or prostatitis (in males), referral to a urologist is warranted to evaluate the lower urinary tract.

### Laboratory Analysis

- **Urine Dipstick**: a clean-catch, midstream urine sample is collected. Positive findings include:
  - **Leukocyte esterase**: indicative of pyuria (white blood cells in urine)
  - **Nitrites**: most UTIs will also show positive nitrites if bacteria can reduce nitrate to nitrite (Enterococcus and Staphylococcus saprophyticus, which are nitrate negative, will not produce nitrites)
- **Microscopic Urine Analysis**: shows WBCs, bacteria, and sometimes RBCs. White blood cell casts can also be seen in patients with pyelonephritis (casts only happen when there is disease within the kidney tubules).
- **Urine Culture**: routine for patients with pyelonephritis and complicated cystitis. It can help customize antibiotic regimen and determine resistance in recurrent UTIs or in UTIs that do not respond to antibiotic therapy.
- **Blood Tests**: pyuria and bacteriuria are prominent; hematuria may be observed. WBC casts indicate involvement of the kidney parenchyma. If a patient with pyelonephritis appears toxic, a complete blood count and blood cultures should be performed. Leukocytosis (elevated white blood cell count) is often present in patients with pyelonephritis.

### Radiologic Imaging

A CT scan or ultrasound of the kidneys should be ordered when:
- A patient with cystitis still has clinical symptoms after 48 to 72 hours of appropriate antibiotic therapy
- The patient has pyelonephritis with severe symptoms such as high fevers, low blood pressure, or signs of instability
- A renal structural obstruction or abnormality is suspected as the cause for the UTI, as in small children, or patients with renal stones

**Computed Tomography**: CT scans can detect pyelonephritis or underlying anatomic abnormalities (horseshoe kidneys or abnormal ureters, stones, renal or perinephric abscesses, renal papillary necrosis, or obstructing tumors). Pyelonephritis on a CT scan shows striated parenchymal enhancement, an alternation of dark and light lines in the kidney (Figure 2).

**Renal Ultrasound**: preferred imaging method for patients who cannot have exposure to a contrast agent or radiation (i.e., pregnant patients or patients with underlying chronic kidney disease in whom IV contrast may be hazardous and cause worsening of renal function). It is used to exclude stones and other structural abnormalities.

**Retrograde Cystogram**: for patients with recurrent pyelonephritis. A retrograde cystogram may be performed to evaluate for vesicoureteral reflux (VUR), in which urine abnormally refluxes from the bladder up the ureters into the renal collecting system. A catheter is placed into the urinary bladder, and radiographic contrast is instilled to attempt to fill the urinary bladder to maximum capacity. Fluoroscopic evaluation can show reflux if the contrast goes into the ureters. Nuclear medicine scans can also diagnose VUR.

### Table 1: Cystitis vs Pyelonephritis

| | Cystitis | Pyelonephritis |
|---|---|---|
| **Vital signs** | Afebrile, normal | Fever, occasionally hypotension if sepsis |
| **Signs/symptoms** | Dysuria, urinary frequency, urinary urgency | Cystitis symptoms + flank pain, CVA tenderness |
| **Urinalysis** | WBC, bacteria, leukocyte esterase, nitrates | WBC, bacteria, sometimes WBC casts |
| **Imaging** | None if uncomplicated; CT or US if unstable, obstructed, resistant to therapy >48 hours | None if uncomplicated; CT or US if unstable, obstructed, resistant to therapy >48 hours |

## Management (LO4)

Treatment is best organized into uncomplicated cystitis and complicated cystitis, with pyelonephritis included in the complicated category because it is a more severe upper urinary tract infection that requires urine culture and closer attention to severity, risk factors, and the need for hospitalization. Most urinary tract infections are caused by gram-negative uropathogens, especially Escherichia coli, with other important gram-negative organisms including Proteus mirabilis, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Enterobacter cloacae, Serratia marcescens, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. These organisms have intrinsic or acquired resistance and tend to be drug resistant. Persistent fever after appropriate therapy should raise concern for a urologic abscess, severe pyelonephritis may progress to urosepsis, hypotension, multi-organ failure, emphysematous pyelonephritis, or chronic renal scarring.

### Uncomplicated Cystitis

First-line agents: **nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX, or fosfomycin** (Table 2).

- **Nitrofurantoin**: damages bacterial DNA and other macromolecules. Common adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset, and less commonly pulmonary toxicity, hepatotoxicity, and peripheral neuropathy, particularly with prolonged use. May cause urine discoloration (dark brown, like tea or cola — a benign effect).
- **Fosfomycin**: inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by blocking an early cytoplasmic step in peptidoglycan formation. Common adverse effects: diarrhea, nausea, headache. Useful for lower UTI but not appropriate for pyelonephritis (does not achieve adequate renal parenchymal concentrations). Single-dose administration is its main advantage.

### Complicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis

For complicated cystitis with stable patients (no factors such as fever, hypotension, vomiting, unable to eat, or suspected of urinary obstruction), oral antibiotics can be managed within this same broader category because it requires urine culture and closer attention with stable patients without these complications. Empiric oral therapy includes:
- **Sulfamethoxazole/levofloxacin or TMP-SMX**, while patients with systemic illness or inability to tolerate oral therapy may need hospitalization and **IV ceftriaxone** or **IV fluoroquinolone**.

Patients who are persistently febrile, hypotensive, vomiting, unable to eat, or suspected of having urinary obstruction should be hospitalized. If the patient is very ill, has concern for resistant hospital-associated gram-negative pathogens such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa, or has obstruction, broader empiric therapy may be required. Persistent fever after appropriate therapy should raise concern for a perinephric abscess, and severe pyelonephritis may progress to urosepsis, hypotension, multiorgan failure, emphysematous pyelonephritis, or chronic renal scarring.

In pregnancy, lower urinary tract infection is usually treated with agents that have better established safety, such as nitrofurantoin or cephalosporin. SMX-TMP should NOT be used in pregnancy, particularly in the first trimester and near term, due to the risks of birth defects and neonatal complications.

### Table 2: Antibiotic Choice

| Category | Typical Setting | First-line options |
|---|---|---|
| **Uncomplicated cystitis** | Healthy, nonpregnant female without urologic abnormality or major immunocompromising condition | Nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX, Fosfomycin |
| **Complicated cystitis** | Male sex, pregnancy, diabetes, CKD, immunosuppression, recurrent UTI, catheter, structural abnormality | Ciprofloxacin/Levofloxacin, TMP-SMX |
| **Pyelonephritis / severe complicated UTI** | Fever, flank pain, CVA tenderness, systemic illness, inability to tolerate oral intake, hypotension, suspected obstruction | IV Ceftriaxone, IV Ciprofloxacin/Levofloxacin, *Meropenem, *Piperacillin-Tazobactam, *Cefepime (*sepsis w/ shock AND/OR suspicion of Pseudomonas) |

### Prevention of Recurrent Urinary Tract Infections

Recurrent UTIs are defined as 2 or more infections in 6 months or 3 or more infections in 1 year. Prevention should focus on identifying modifiable contributors and reducing future episodes:
- Consider evaluation for sexually transmitted infections when symptoms recur
- Encourage adequate fluid intake
- Avoid spermicides and diaphragms when relevant
- Consider topical vaginal estrogen in postmenopausal patients
- In persistent recurrence despite these measures, antibiotic prophylaxis may be indicated. Many of the same patient factors that define complicated infection, such as anatomic abnormality or impaired host defenses, also matter when considering prevention, because they raise the likelihood of recurrence and treatment failure.

## Summary

- Cystitis is an infection of the lower urinary tract (bladder).
- Pyelonephritis is an upper urinary tract infection (kidneys).
- Cystitis presents with dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain, and hematuria.
- Pyelonephritis presents with symptoms of cystitis plus flank pain, fever, costovertebral angle tenderness, nausea, and vomiting.
- The most common cause of urinary tract infections (UTIs) is E. coli.
- Uncomplicated cystitis occurs in healthy females with no underlying anatomic abnormalities or immunosuppression. It can be diagnosed presumptively by history and physical examination, is normally treated with antibiotics, and urinalysis and cultures are not required.
- Complicated cystitis occurs in healthy individuals with anatomic or other underlying medical conditions, as well as in males. It is diagnosed presumptively with antibiotics such as fluoroquinolones.
- Complicated cystitis and pyelonephritis are treated with a fluoroquinolone, trimethoprim-sulfa, or a third-generation cephalosporin.
- Recurrent UTIs occur 2 or more times in 6 months or 3 or more times per year, and screening for sexually transmitted diseases and other preventive measures should be implemented.

title: Renal Stones

Patient writhing, cannot sit still
Unilateral flank pain radiating to groin
Hematuria with dysuria and urgency

Envelope or dumbbell-shaped urine crystals
