transcript:
# Bladder Cancer

## Learning Objectives

1. Differentiate the clinical presentation of bladder cancer.
2. Explain the pathophysiology of bladder cancer.
3. Interpret clinical and lab findings to properly diagnose bladder cancer.
4. Outline the management plan for bladder cancer.

## Case Connection

RR is a 53-year-old male referred for an abnormal urinalysis obtained during a work physical. He works at a tire factory and undergoes a physical every 2 years. He reports no visible blood in the urine but was told microscopic hematuria is present. He smokes, has smoked 1 to 1.5 packs/day for 30-40 years, and has a poor diet. Findings prompted an MRI to evaluate for bladder cancer.

## What Is Bladder Cancer? (LO #1)

Bladder cancer is one of the most common cancers in the United States and is a major hazard of tobacco smoking.

### Urothelial Carcinoma

The most common type of bladder cancer is **urothelial carcinoma (UCC)**, a malignant tumor derived from the urothelium of the genitourinary tract. It is also called "transitional cell carcinoma."

- Approximately 90% of UCC occurs in the bladder.
- Can occur anywhere along the urothelium-lined urinary tract, from renal pelvis to urethra.
- Most cases are low-grade tumors with a relatively good prognosis.

### Epidemiology

- UCC is the fourth most common cancer in U.S. males and ninth in females.
- Affects approximately 75,000 people per year.
- Male predominance (3:1).
- Mostly seen in patients aged 50 to 80 years.
- Ninety percent of UCCs occur in the bladder.

## How Do Patients With Bladder Cancer Present? (LO #1)

- Most common symptom: **painless gross hematuria** (visible blood in the urine).
- Hematuria may also be microscopic, detected on routine urinalysis.
- UCC is diagnosed in up to 20% of patients with gross hematuria.
- Second most common cause of gross hematuria after cystitis.
- Hematuria is often intermittent and may be accompanied by voiding symptoms:
  - Dysuria (painful urination)
  - Nocturia (excessive urination at night)
  - Increased urinary frequency
- Less commonly: decreased force of stream, incomplete voiding if the tumor blocks the bladder neck or ureteral orifice.
- Flank pain may occur from hydronephrosis and renal obstruction.

## What Is the Pathophysiology of Urothelial Cancer? (LO #2)

While UCC is strongly linked to tobacco smoking, there are other causes as well.

### Risk Factors

- **Tobacco smoking** — risk of UCC is increased 3- to 7-fold in patients who smoke; risk depends on duration of smoking.
- **Occupational exposure to arylamines, used in dyes** — risk may occur 15 to 40 years after first exposure; associated with UCC including chemical workers, metal workers, painters, cement workers, and those who manufacture carpets and paint.
- **Cyclophosphamide** — immunosuppressive drug; known to cause hemorrhagic cystitis (inflammation of the bladder with bleeding); also increases risk of UCC.
- **Pelvic radiation** — particularly from pelvic radiotherapy for other malignancies (e.g., prostate, cervical, or rectal cancer); risk factor for developing bladder cancer, typically following a latency period of several decades.
- **HPV (human papillomavirus)** — associated with UCC; includes illicit opiate use, arsenic in drinking water, working in a rubber/tire factory; also a cause of most cervical cancer.

### Genetics

UCC has a minor familial inheritance. Tumors are commonly associated with loss-of-function mutations in the **TP53 and RB tumor suppressor genes** (high-grade).

## Pathologic Types of Urothelial Cancer

The most important prognostic factor for UCC is the degree to which it invades structures below the basement membrane (e.g., lamina propria). Invasive UCC invades below the basement membrane, while noninvasive neoplasms do not.

For invasive UCC, the prognosis worsens with a greater **depth of invasion**, as the tumor extends into the submucosa, then progressively into the muscle, perivesical fat, adjacent organs (vagina, prostate, or bowel), or into the abdomen or abdominal wall (see Staging in the Diagnosis section, below).

There are two precursor noninvasive lesions that can lead to invasive UCC: papillary lesions (more common) and flat lesions. Both are noninvasive (above the basement membrane). The prognosis is much better with these precursor lesions, and their treatment is different from more invasive cancers.

## Histopathology of Urothelial Cancer

### Noninvasive Papillary Lesions

These include a variety of lesions, ranging from benign papillomas to malignant neoplasms that later progress to invasion. You don't need to know all the details of these lesions, but realize that most have a common architecture, with fibrovascular stalks (forming a papilla) and epithelial cells of varying atypia, depending on the specific grade. A low-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma with fine, nonbranching fibrovascular cores is shown in Figure 2.

### Noninvasive Flat Lesions

Unlike the papillary lesions, these are flat (not stalked). The two subtypes are dysplastic lesions and **carcinoma in situ (CIS)**. CIS refers to epithelial lesions that have the cytologic features of malignancy but are confined to the epithelium.

### Invasive Urothelial Carcinoma

Invasive UCC extends through the basement membrane into the lamina propria and underlying structures. Shows a more advanced (high-grade) urothelial carcinoma with dyscohesive, atypical cells with large nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and numerous mitotic figures. Similar cells are seen at higher power.

## What Is the Pathophysiology of Squamous Cell Bladder Carcinoma? (LO #2)

Squamous cell cancer of the bladder is much less common than UCC (only 5% of cases), and the prognosis is generally poor. The majority are high-grade and tend to have invaded the muscle at the time of diagnosis. These carcinomas vary widely in terms of histological appearance, ranging from well-differentiated to poorly differentiated.

### Clinical Correlation: Schistosomiasis

Infection with **Schistosoma haematobium** is a risk factor for squamous cell bladder cancer in patients from some countries in the Middle East and sub-Saharan Africa. Note that S. haematobium is not a risk factor for UCC. Schistosoma haematobium is a parasitic flatworm (fluke) and the cause of urinary schistosomiasis, endemic in parts of Africa and the Middle East. The parasite's ova lodge in the walls of the bladder and ureters, inducing a chronic granulomatous inflammatory response. The urothelium responds to this chronic stress by undergoing squamous metaplasia. This chronic inflammation is a major risk factor for the development of squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the bladder.

## How Do We Diagnose Bladder Cancer? (LO #3)

Many patients are asymptomatic, and their condition is first detected when an office urinalysis shows hematuria. The first step in diagnosis is to rule out common causes of hematuria that have very different treatments compared to cancers.

- Rule out cystitis (bladder infection) by checking for white blood cells and bacteria, which will be absent in malignancy.
- Check for proteinuria and casts that might indicate glomerular disease.

### Cystoscopy and Urine Cytology

- **Cystoscopy** is the gold standard for the initial diagnosis of bladder cancer.
- Combined with **urine cytology**, which may detect lesions missed at cystoscopy or upper urinary tract lesions (ureter, renal pelvis).
- Initial cystoscopy is typically done in an outpatient setting with a flexible cystoscope.
- Visible tumors are biopsied or resected transurethrally to establish histopathologic diagnosis and assess depth of invasion (mucosa, submucosa, muscularis).

### Radiology

- Upper urinary tract can be imaged by CT or MRI with intravenous contrast.
- MRI may also be used to stage primary bladder cancer.

### Staging

Once UCC is detected by biopsy, the workup proceeds to determine the degree of extension and metastatic spread, guiding treatment and prognosis.

- Distant metastasis may be assessed by CT of the chest/abdomen, MRI, or radionuclide skeletal imaging.
- UCC is staged 0 to 4 based on extension, lymph node involvement, and metastasis.
- **Stage 0**: tumor confined to the epithelium (in situ).
- **Stage 4**: distant metastasis present.
- Most patients present with lower-stage tumors (0 to 1), likely because early hematuria triggers early evaluation.
- Metastasis is the primary characteristic of stage 4 bladder cancer.

## What Are the Management Options and Prognoses for Bladder Cancers? (LO #4)

Management and prognosis of urothelial cancer depend on stage and whether it has invaded the detrusor muscle or spread to lymph nodes and beyond.

### Urothelial Carcinoma

**Treatment.** For superficial disease confined to the bladder epithelium (e.g., papillary and flat, noninvasive lesions):

- **Cystoscopy with local resection** is used, followed by instillation of chemotherapy agents (usually gemcitabine, an antimetabolite) into the bladder (intravesicular therapy).
- This includes **bacillus Calmette-Guerin (BCG)** — a live attenuated form of *Mycobacterium bovis*, which contains live mycobacteria that do not cause clinical tuberculosis but induce inflammation that helps treat superficial UCC.
- BCG induces inflammation that helps eradicate the tumor in treating superficial UCC.

For higher-stage tumors that have invaded into the bladder muscle (muscle invasive bladder cancer), the best treatment is chemotherapy (neoadjuvant) followed by **radical cystectomy** (removal of the bladder). Radical cystectomy also involves removal of the prostate, seminal vesicles, and proximal urethra in males. In females, it involves removal of the uterus, fallopian tubes, ovaries, and anterior vagina.

- Immunotherapy (anti-PD-L1 inhibitors) are used in patients who are not surgical candidates.
- New gene therapy **erdafitinib** is a fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR) inhibitor used for invasive and metastatic disease if a specific mutation for FGFR is present.

**Prognosis.** Outcome and treatment of UCC depend on both tumor grade and stage. The tumor grade is determined by its histology.

- Five-year survival is roughly 90% if disease is localized (stage 1).
- Roughly 70% to 50% (year-survival) in regional disease (stage 2).
- Roughly 30% to 40% if local extension (stage 3).
- 5-year survival is likely to have <5% if there is metastasis (stage 4).
- Within each stage, prognosis becomes poorer with advancing tumor histologic grade.

### Squamous Cell Cancer

**Treatment.** Squamous cell carcinoma is generally treated with **radical cystectomy** because it is often found at later stages.

**Prognosis.** Poor prognosis, with a 5-year survival rate of 33% to 48%.

## Summary

- The most common form of bladder cancer is urothelial carcinoma (UCC) (also called transitional cell carcinoma).
- UCC occurs in the urothelium of the genitourinary tract, most frequently in the bladder, but also in the renal pelvis, ureters, and parts of the urethra.
- Squamous cell carcinoma is a less common form.
- Risk factors for urothelial cancer include long-term smoking, exposure to industrial toxins such as arylamines (older age), smoking, exposure to industrial toxins such as cyclophosphamide.
- UCC is associated with loss-of-function mutations in TP53 and RB tumor suppressor genes.
- The most important prognostic factor for UCC is the basement membrane status.
- There are two precursor lesions that can lead to invasive UCC: noninvasive papillary carcinoma and noninvasive carcinoma in situ; both are noninvasive (above the basement membrane). The prognosis is much better with these precursor lesions, and their treatment is different from more invasive cancers.
- Bladder cancer is diagnosed primarily by cystoscopy with biopsy.
- Disease typically begins with local resection plus intravesical chemotherapy or BCG immunotherapy; muscle-invasive disease is managed with radical cystectomy and chemotherapy.
- Systemic disease is managed through radical cystectomy and chemotherapy for metastases.
- The new gene therapy erdafitinib is a fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR) inhibitor used for invasive and metastatic disease if a specific FGFR mutation is present.

title: Micturition
buzzwords: Two-phase process controlled by autonomic and somatic nerves
Coordinated activity of a hollow muscular organ and its outlets
Switch-like brainstem reflex modulated by cortical input
Failure causes incontinence, urgency, or incomplete emptying
A-delta stretch afferents signal wall tension to spinal cord
Storage uses sympathetic T10-L2; voiding uses parasympathetic S2-S4
Detrusor contraction with simultaneous internal and external sphincter relaxation
PAG and pontine micturition center coordinate bladder emptying
transcript:
# Micturition

## Learning Objectives

1. Apply the storage-voiding model to predict detrusor and sphincter states from neural output patterns.
2. Analyze the neural control loop from bladder stretch afferents to cortical modulation in coordinating micturition.

## Case Connection

A 62-year-old male presents to an outpatient urology clinic with urinary urgency and incontinence episodes lasting several months. The case prompts consideration of how storage versus voiding phases function, and how sympathetic, parasympathetic, and somatic control mechanisms account for symptoms.

## Storage Versus Voiding Phases (LO1)

Micturition (urination) is a cycle of two distinct functional phases. The bladder must serve two opposing demands: act as a compliant reservoir during filling and transform into an efficient pump during emptying. Both require coordinated muscular and neural activity.

### Storage Phase Functions

- Urine is produced continuously by the kidneys.
- The bladder accommodates increasing urine volume through increased compliance, allowing filling without uncomfortable pressure rises.
- The **detrusor muscle** (smooth muscle of the bladder wall) remains relaxed to allow low-pressure filling.
- The **internal urethral sphincter (IUS)** and **external urethral sphincter (EUS)** are contracted to maintain continence.
- The guarding reflex reinforces sphincter tone as bladder pressure rises or with abdominal pressure spikes (coughing, exercise).
- Storage is an active, sustained state, not passive flow retention.

### Voiding Phase Functions

- The detrusor contracts strongly to drive bladder emptying.
- The IUS opens passively as the bladder neck descends during detrusor contraction.
- The EUS is actively released by withdrawal of somatic (voluntary) motor activity.
- Detrusor contraction and sphincter relaxation must occur simultaneously. **Dyssynergia** (failure of this coordination) results in incomplete emptying or obstructed voiding.
- Voiding is voluntarily initiated; once started, the reflex is self-sustaining until the bladder is empty.

The transition between phases is not gradual. It is a switch-like neural event driven by brainstem circuits and modulated by cortical input.

## Neural Control of Detrusor and Sphincter (LO1)

Three pathways coordinate the bladder system: sympathetic, parasympathetic, and somatic.

### Sympathetic Pathway (Storage)

- Sympathetic fibers originate from spinal levels T10-L2 and travel via the hypogastric nerve.
- Norepinephrine release acts on two receptor subtypes:
  - **β3 adrenergic receptors** on the detrusor: raise cAMP, promote smooth muscle relaxation for low-pressure filling.
  - **α1 adrenergic receptors** on the IUS: Gq/IP3 signaling drives smooth muscle contraction and maintains outlet resistance.

### Parasympathetic Pathway (Voiding)

- Parasympathetic fibers originate from sacral levels S2-S4 and travel via the pelvic nerve.
- Acetylcholine acts on muscarinic receptors:
  - **M3 muscarinic receptors**: Gq/PLC signaling raises intracellular calcium, driving sustained detrusor contraction.
  - **M2 muscarinic receptors**: amplify contraction by suppressing cAMP-mediated relaxation.

### Somatic Neural Pathway (Voluntary Control)

- Somatic motor control of the EUS is carried by the pudendal nerve, arising from Onuf's nucleus at S2-S4.
- The pudendal nerve releases ACh at nicotinic receptors on the striated muscle of the EUS, driving contraction during storage.
- During voiding, pudendal activity is actively suppressed, allowing the EUS to relax.
- The EUS is the primary site of voluntary continence.

### Table 1: Autonomic Micturition Pathways

| Phase | Pathway | Target | Receptor | Signaling | Effect |
|-------|---------|--------|----------|-----------|--------|
| Storage | Sympathetic (T10-L2) | Detrusor | β3 | ↑cAMP | Relaxation |
| Storage | Sympathetic (T10-L2) | Internal sphincter | α1 | Gq/IP3 | Contraction |
| Storage | Somatic pudendal (S2-S4) | External sphincter | Nicotinic | — | Contraction |
| Voiding | Parasympathetic (S2-S4) | Detrusor | M3 | Gq/PLC/Ca²⁺ | Contraction |
| Voiding | Parasympathetic (S2-S4) | Detrusor | M2 | ↓cAMP | Amplifies contraction |
| Voiding | Withdrawal of autonomic tone | Sphincters | — | — | Relaxation |

## Predicting States From Neural Output (LO1)

The storage-voiding model predicts the functional state of each muscle group from a known pattern of neural output, and anticipates drug, nerve injury, and disease effects.

### Predicting Storage State

When sympathetic output from T10-L2 dominates, norepinephrine binds β3 receptors on the detrusor (raising cAMP, smooth muscle relaxation) and α1 receptors on the IUS (Gq/IP3, contraction). High pudendal nerve activity maintains EUS contraction via nicotinic ACh receptors. The net result: a relaxed, compliant bladder with a closed, high-resistance outlet.

### Predicting Voiding State

When parasympathetic output from S2-S4 dominates, ACh binds M3 receptors on the detrusor (Gq/PLC, raised intracellular calcium, sustained contraction). M2 receptor activation amplifies this by suppressing cAMP-mediated relaxation. Pudendal activity is suppressed, allowing EUS relaxation, and withdrawal of sympathetic α1 tone allows the IUS to open. The net result: a contracting detrusor with an open, low-resistance outlet.

### Clinical Reasoning

A key insight: voiding requires active suppression of storage signals, not merely activation of voiding signals. Incomplete suppression of sympathetic or pudendal activity leads to detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia and incomplete bladder emptying.

## Neural Control Loop (LO2)

Neural control of micturition is a closed-loop system that integrates sensory information from the bladder, processes it through brainstem circuits, and subjects it to cortical modulation. This loop determines when the switch from storage to voiding occurs, explaining why voiding is both reflexive and voluntary.

### Stretch Afferents and Signal Initiation

As the bladder fills, tension in the detrusor wall increases. This mechanical stretch is detected by **Aδ (A-delta) myelinated afferent fibers** in the bladder wall, which encode bladder volume and wall tension. Aδ fibers transmit signals rapidly to the spinal cord via the pelvic nerve, ascending to supraspinal centers to initiate the voiding reflex.

### Brainstem Centers — PAG and Pontine Micturition Center

Ascending Aδ signals first reach the **periaqueductal gray (PAG)** in the midbrain. The PAG acts as a relay and integrator: it receives bladder fullness signals alongside descending input from the prefrontal cortex and limbic areas. Rather than directly triggering voiding, the PAG gates the voiding signal based on social and contextual appropriateness. When conditions are appropriate, the PAG activates the **pontine micturition center (PMC)**, also called Barrington's nucleus, in the dorsal pons.

The PMC is the master switch for micturition. When activated, it simultaneously sends excitatory signals to sacral parasympathetic neurons (triggering detrusor contraction) and inhibitory signals to Onuf's nucleus (suppressing pudendal activity and relaxing the EUS).

### Cortical Modulation and Guarding Reflex

The prefrontal cortex and anterior cingulate cortex exert tonic inhibitory control over the PAG-PMC pathway during storage, raising the threshold for voiding and allowing the bladder to fill beyond the point at which stretch afferents first fire. Conscious awareness of bladder fullness emerges as Aδ signals reach the PAG and are relayed to cortical areas, producing the sensation of urgency. The decision to void or defer is a cortical function influenced by social context and prior experience.

The **guarding reflex** reinforces continence during storage by increasing EUS contraction in response to rising bladder pressure or sudden intra-abdominal pressure increases. This reflex is mediated by a spinal loop: bladder afferents activate pudendal motor neurons via interneurons in the sacral cord, independent of supraspinal control.

### Clinical Correlation

The guarding reflex operates as a local protective mechanism even when cortical or brainstem control is disrupted, explaining why some continence is preserved after incomplete spinal cord injuries.

## Case Conclusion

In the 62-year-old man, urinary urgency may reflect heightened afferent activity or impaired cortical inhibition of the PAG-PMC loop. Incontinence episodes suggest impaired sphincter contraction during storage or detrusor overactivity, overwhelming sphincter resistance and producing urgency and leakage.

## Summary

- Micturition consists of two distinct phases: storage (low-pressure bladder filling with sphincters contracted) and voiding (coordinated detrusor contraction with sphincter relaxation), each requiring precise neural coordination.
- The storage phase is maintained by sympathetic dominance (T10-L2 hypogastric nerve): β3 receptor activation relaxes the detrusor, α1 receptor activation contracts the IUS, and pudendal nerve activity (S2-S4) contracts the EUS.
- The voiding phase is driven by parasympathetic dominance (S2-S4 pelvic nerve): M3 and M2 muscarinic receptor activation contracts the detrusor, while pudendal inhibition and IUS relaxation open the outlet.
- The neural control loop runs from Aδ stretch afferents → PAG (midbrain) → pontine micturition center (PMC), which coordinates simultaneous detrusor contraction and sphincter relaxation; cortical input modulates the voiding threshold and enables voluntary deferral.
- The guarding reflex — a spinal loop reinforcing EUS contraction during bladder filling — provides a local continence mechanism independent of supraspinal control.

transcript:
# Bladder Dysfunction: Urinary Incontinence and Neurogenic Bladder

## Learning Objectives

1. Differentiate the clinical presentation of bladder dysfunction.
2. Explain the pathophysiology of bladder dysfunction.
3. Interpret clinical and lab findings to properly diagnose bladder dysfunction.
4. Outline the management plan for bladder dysfunction.

## Case Connection

MU is a 64-year-old female who comes to the office and occasionally complains of urine leakage when she coughs, especially when laughing or sneezing. She also stops for a few seconds and then continues. She has avoided social events because of urine leakage. A little urine leaks out. MU has three children, all born vaginally with no complications. She has no other medical history. When asked about other urinary symptoms, she says she has none. Vital signs and pelvic exam are unremarkable, and results from a urinalysis are normal.

## What Is Bladder Dysfunction? (LO#1)

Bladder dysfunction occurs when normal mechanisms for bladder storage or emptying are impaired due to structural or neurologic causes. It includes **urinary incontinence** and **neurogenic bladder**, which manifest as lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) such as urinary urgency, frequency, retention, incontinence, or nocturia. Complications include recurrent urinary tract infections, skin infection/rash, and psychosocial distress.

**Urinary incontinence** is the involuntary leakage of urine. It commonly affects pregnant women and affects up to 50% of women and 25% of men over the age of 65.

- *Clinical correlation:* Men with bladder outlet obstruction (BOO) from benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) commonly experience urinary incontinence.

**Neurogenic bladder** occurs when there is damage to the brain, spinal cord, nerve roots, or peripheral nerves resulting in either a flaccid or spastic bladder leading to urinary incontinence with associated symptoms and complications.

- *Clinical correlation:* Patients with strokes, brain tumors, spinal cord lesions/injuries, skeletal spine disorders, demyelinating neurologic diseases, and some metabolic diseases such as diabetes and pernicious anemia are at risk for developing a neurogenic bladder. The specific dysfunction pattern depends on the location of the lesion(s) or injury.

## Clinical Presentations and Pathophysiology of Urinary Incontinence (LO#1,2)

### Types, Causes, and Symptoms of Incontinence

Three main forms: **Overflow**, **Stress**, and **Urge** ("Just can't hold it any more," "Too little sphincter tone," "Too much activity").

**Stress incontinence** occurs when there is leakage of small volumes of urine during increased abdominal pressure (e.g., laughing, coughing, sneezing). This is due to the lack of mechanical support of the urethra by the weakened pelvic muscles and is most commonly seen in women with a history of multiple vaginal deliveries. It may also develop after prostate surgery.

**Overflow incontinence** occurs when there is chronic bladder overdistension and urinary retention due to weak detrusor contractions (detrusor underactivity) or bladder outlet obstruction. Eventually, the bladder volume and pressure overcome the urethral resistance, resulting in continuous or intermittent dribbling and postvoid leakage. Other symptoms include weak urinary stream, hesitancy and nocturia. Detrusor underactivity can result from aging; neurogenic bladder (see below); medications with anticholinergic properties or pelvic nerve injury from surgery or trauma. Bladder outlet obstruction, which eventually leads to overdistension and overflow incontinence, can result from urethral compression by BPH, prostate cancer, urethral stricture, calculi, pelvic tumors (e.g., fibroids), or pelvic organ prolapse (e.g., cystocele).

**Urge incontinence** is urinary leakage occurring in conjunction with overactive bladder due to **detrusor hyperactivity**. Individuals typically experience a sudden, intense urge to urinate from bladder spasms, often followed by involuntary urine release. Urge incontinence is common with aging and can be the result of neurogenic bladder. Additionally, any irritation to the bladder can cause an overactive bladder and so urge incontinence. Examples include urinary tract infections, bladder stones and caffeine.

### Exacerbating Factors

- **α-1 blocking drugs** may excessively relax the internal urethral sphincter and cause/worsen stress incontinence (terazosin, doxazosin used for BPH).
- **Diuretics** may cause rapid increases in bladder urine volume. Antidepressants, antihypertensives, and NSAIDs are also associated with incontinence.
- **Cystitis** worsens incontinence by increasing bladder irritability.
- **Immobility** may make reaching the bathroom difficult.

## Clinical Presentation and Pathophysiology of Neurogenic Bladder (LO#1,2)

Neurogenic bladder is a condition in which there is **bladder overactivity** or **underactivity** either due to nerve damage/disease or impaired responsiveness of the bladder to neurologic stimuli. A neurogenic bladder develops when there is a disruption anywhere in the micturition reflex involving the CNS (cortex, brainstem, spinal cord), or PNS (nerve roots or peripheral nerves). The micturition reflex begins with the detection of stretch and fullness by the bladder mechanoreceptors and ends with detrusor contraction. This process involves sensory and motor systems, both somatic (voluntary) and autonomic (involuntary). Refer to the Micturition Brick to review the micturition reflex.

### Forms of Neurogenic Bladder

Neurogenic bladder can take two forms:

**Spastic (overactive) bladder.** The bladder has many involuntary contractions and the bladder volume is usually low. It often leads to **urge incontinence**. Paradoxically, it may lead to **urinary retention** due to the bladder failing to fully empty and lack of voiding coordination between the somatically controlled external urethral sphincter and the parasympathetically controlled contracting bladder. This is called detrusor sphincter dyssynergy and is often seen with **upper motor neuron** lesions, such as multiple sclerosis, transverse myelitis, and suprasacral spinal cord injuries.

**Flaccid (underactive) bladder.** The bladder is hypotonic and there are no contractions. It leads to **overflow incontinence**. This may be caused by peripheral nerve injury as seen with diabetes and B12 deficiency, or sacral/infrasacral spinal cord injury such as conus medullaris syndrome and cauda equina syndrome. It may also be due to a bladder that has become overdistended due to drugs or congenital conditions like posterior urethral valves.

### Etiology

**Brain.** Brain injuries (stroke, trauma, tumors) to the micturition center inhibit voluntary control of micturition but leave the involuntary micturition reflex in the spinal cord intact. This leads to uncontrolled voiding and an overactive bladder.

- *Clinical correlation:* Stroke initially affects bladder function due to degenerative disc disease causing cord compression; spinal cord injury from trauma; demyelinating systemic diseases; tumors or infections. The nerves that are part of the micturition reflex enter and leave the spinal cord between T10 and L2.

**Spinal Cord.** Spinal cord damage can be due to degenerative disc disease causing cord compression; spinal cord injury from trauma; demyelinating systemic diseases; tumors or infections. The sympathetic autonomic nerves at T10–L2 and the parasympathetic autonomic nerves S2–S4 supplied level → spastic bladder may also be due to a bladder that has been hypertrophied and has been trying to contract against an outflow obstruction, like BPH.

In **flaccid (underactive) bladder**, the bladder is hypotonic and there are no contractions. It leads to **overflow incontinence**.

### Spinal cord damage may be due to:

- **Spinal cord compression:** posterior intervertebral disc herniation, osteoarthritis, and spinal stenosis. If these diseases affect vertebral levels above L2 or suprasacral spinal cord levels, spastic neurogenic bladder will result. If lower vertebral levels are involved, conus medullaris syndrome or cauda equina syndrome may result. Less common causes of cord compression include compression fractures due to osteoporosis, osteomyelitis, spinal tuberculosis, and metastatic tumors to the vertebrae.
- **Spinal cord injury (SCI):** Spinal cord trauma occurs in over 12,000 people annually in the United States. The most common causes are motor vehicle accidents, falls, gunshot wounds, or sports injuries. Acutely, SCI causes both flaccid sphincter and detrusor activity, with eventual progression to spasticity or emptying. Depending on the level of the injury and the extent of recovery, the bladder is freed from higher centric nervous system controls and detrusor sphincter dyssynergy. Some of the findings may improve with time, but in other cases patients are left with permanent deficits.
- **Multiple sclerosis:** This is a systemic demyelinating disease that involves the loss of the myelin sheath surrounding nerves. If so, the nerves of the micturition reflex are involved, neurogenic bladder will result. The disease may also affect bladder function indirectly with sensory and gait disturbances.

**Peripheral Nerves.** Diabetes mellitus and HIV/AIDS are common causes of **peripheral neuropathy** that can result in neurogenic bladder. These disorders interrupt the nerves to the bladder and can cause impaired sensation of bladder fullness and painless distension. The bladder in patients with peripheral neuropathy contracts or flaccid, depending on the injured bladder emptying.

Less common causes of peripheral neuropathy are Guillain-Barré syndrome, severe genital herpes infection, pernicious anemia, organophosphate, heavy metal poisoning, and polio.

- *Clinical correlation:* Patients with diabetes may have autonomic neuropathy, causing impaired muscle underactivity and lack of sensation when the bladder is full, and subsequently overflow urine leakage.

## How Do We Diagnose Urinary Incontinence and Neurogenic Bladder? (LO#3)

After conducting a thorough history and physical exam to look for secondary causes (e.g., diabetes, Parkinson disease), we then rule out UTI using urinalysis and, if needed, urine culture. Male patients should be assessed for BPH with a digital rectal exam, since BPH can present with similar symptoms (nocturia, urinary frequency, hesitancy, incomplete voiding).

### Specific Tests

**Postvoid residual (PVR) test:** The patient urinates completely, then a Foley catheter is placed to determine the amount of urine left in the bladder. Values greater than 50 to 100 mL may indicate abnormal voiding. The PVR can also be estimated by ultrasound, avoiding the catheter.

**Urodynamic studies** performed by a urologist can measure urine flow rates, bladder capacity, and detrusor pressure generation.

## How Do We Manage Incontinence and Neurogenic Bladder? (LO#4)

Therapeutic options range from exercises to surgery. Many patients may benefit from lifestyle modifications: reducing daily fluid intake, avoiding drinking large volumes at one time, limiting alcohol and caffeine, weight reduction if obese, smoking cessation. Consider other medical conditions (diabetes) and medications (alpha-1 blockers, diuretics).

### Exercises

**Pelvic floor (Kegel) exercises** may be useful in stress and urge incontinence to strengthen pelvic floor muscles.

**Bladder retraining** for overflow (timed voiding every 2–3 hours) and urge incontinence (progressively increase time between voids to 3–4 hours).

### Devices

- **Pads and diapers:** noninvasive, mild incontinence; risk of skin breakdown.
- **Pessaries:** vaginal devices supporting female stress incontinence.
- **Urinary catheters:** indwelling Foley or intermittent for retention or skin breakdown; suprapubic catheter as alternative.

### Medications

Medications can modulate micturition by acting on detrusor contractility and/or bladder outlet resistance (urethra/prostate). Think about matching the drug to the type of dysfunction.

- **"Too much squeeze":** detrusor overactivity → urge incontinence from spastic bladder → relax the detrusor muscle (antimuscarinics, β3-agonist).
- **"Too little squeeze":** detrusor underactivity/flaccid bladder → stimulate detrusor contraction (cholinergic agonist).
- **"Too much outlet resistance":** often BPH-related in men → relax bladder neck/prostate smooth muscle (α1-blockers).
- **"Too little outlet resistance":** often in women with hypermobile bladder neck → support pelvic floor exercises ± increase smooth muscle contraction of the bladder neck (α1-agonists).

**Topical vaginal estrogen** (women): Postmenopausal women with urinary urgency/frequency and mild incontinence may be exacerbated by genitourinary syndrome of menopause (GSM)—atrophy of the vaginal and periurethral tissues. Estrogen restores urogenital epithelium and reduces UTIs.

**Anticholinergics (antimuscarinics):** For urge incontinence/overactive bladder and spastic neurogenic bladder characterized by detrusor overactivity. Antimuscarinics reduce involuntary bladder contractions by blocking M3 receptors on the detrusor muscle, decreasing bladder contractility and increasing bladder capacity. Common: **oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin, darifenacin**. ADRs: dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, urinary retention, cognitive effects; avoid narrow-angle glaucoma.

**β3-agonist – Mirabegron:** Treats overactive bladder/urge incontinence by stimulating β3 receptors in the detrusor during the storage phase, promoting detrusor relaxation and increasing bladder capacity. Avoids anticholinergic side effects, but may cause hypertension, headache, tachycardia/palpitations.

**Cholinergic therapy – Bethanechol:** Used when the dominant problem is insufficient detrusor contraction. Bethanechol, a muscarinic agonist, stimulates M3 receptors to increase detrusor contractility and promote bladder emptying. ADRs: diarrhea/cramping, salivation, sweat, bronchospasm, bradycardia. Contraindicated in mechanical obstruction.

**α1-blockers (men):** For men with BPH-related obstructive symptoms contributing to urgency/frequency and overflow leakage. α1-blockers reduce outlet resistance by relaxing smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck, improving flow and complete emptying. Common: **tamsulosin, alfuzosin, doxazosin, prazosin**. ADRs: orthostatic hypotension, dizziness, syncope.

- *Clinical correlation:* In many women, incontinence is often a "support problem" (pelvic floor/urethral hypermobility + estrogen-deficient tissues) → first-line is pelvic floor strengthening ± topical estrogen. In many men, incontinence is often an "outlet problem" (prostate-related obstruction) → target bladder outlet resistance (BPH physiology) first.

### Medication Summary Table

| Class | Examples | MOA | Indication | ADRs |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Topical vaginal estrogen | Vaginal estradiol / conjugated estrogen (topical) | Restores urogenital epithelium and urethral mucosal coaptation; improves GSM-related urinary symptoms | Postmenopausal women with GSM + urgency/frequency + mild incontinence | Local irritation/discharge; precaution in estrogen-dependent cancer history |
| Anticholinergics (antimuscarinics) | Oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin, darifenacin | Block M3 → ↓ detrusor overactivity, ↑ bladder capacity | Urge incontinence / OAB, spastic neurogenic bladder | Dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, urinary retention, cognitive effects; avoid narrow-angle glaucoma |
| β3-agonist | Mirabegron | β3 activation → detrusor relaxation during storage | Urge incontinence / OAB (esp. anticholinergic intolerance) | Hypertension, headache, tachycardia/palpitations (less common) |
| Cholinergic (muscarinic agonist) | Bethanechol | M3 stimulation → ↑ detrusor contraction → promotes bladder emptying | Urinary retention/overflow from hypotonic/flaccid bladder (selected neurogenic bladder) | Diarrhea/cramping, salivation, sweat, bronchospasm, bradycardia |
| α1-blockers (men, BPH-focused) | Tamsulosin, alfuzosin, doxazosin, prazosin | α1 blockade → relax prostate/bladder neck smooth muscle → ↓ outlet resistance | Men with BPH-related obstructive symptoms contributing to urgency/frequency and/or overflow | Orthostatic hypotension/dizziness/syncope; ejaculatory dysfunction (tamsulosin) |

### Surgery

There are surgical options for some types of incontinence.

- **Stress incontinence:** procedures that increase urethral outlet resistance, including sling procedures, periurethral bulking therapy, and the artificial urinary sphincter.
- **Urge incontinence:** procedures that improve bladder capacity such as sacral neuromodulation, botulinum toxin injections, or bladder augmentation.

## Summary

- Urinary incontinence is the involuntary leakage of urine and can be classified as overflow, stress, urge, or mixed types, each with distinct presentations, pathophysiology, and causes.
- Neurogenic bladder results from nerve damage or impaired bladder responsiveness, disrupting the micturition reflex and presenting as either spastic (overactive) or flaccid (underactive) bladder.
- Common etiologies for bladder dysfunction include brain injuries, spinal cord damage, peripheral neuropathies (e.g., diabetes), bladder outlet obstruction, and chronic bladder distention.
- Diagnosis involves thorough history and physical examination, urinalysis to rule out UTI, postvoid residual testing, and urodynamic studies.
- Management strategies include lifestyle modifications, pelvic floor exercises, bladder retraining, devices (pads, pessaries, catheters), medications (anticholinergics, β3-agonists, cholinergic agonists, α1-blockers, topical estrogen), and surgery.
- Surgical interventions may be considered for refractory cases, with options varying according to the type of incontinence (e.g., sling procedures for stress incontinence, neuromodulation for urge incontinence).

## Review Questions

1. A 56-year-old female complains of leakage of urine whenever she laughs. This is causing her embarrassment and significant emotional distress whenever she goes out with friends. She also has incontinence when she coughs but no other triggers. What is the most likely diagnosis? (Functional incontinence / Neurogenic bladder / Overflow incontinence / Stress incontinence / Urge incontinence)
2. Which of the following spinal cord lesions would likely result in a flaccid neurogenic bladder and overflow incontinence?
3. A 52-year-old female patient presents with frequent small volume voiding, urgency and nocturia. Urinalysis is negative. The patient has tried nonpharmacologic measures to no avail. What medication would you consider prescribing? (Bethanechol / Mirabegron / Oxybutynin / Tamsulosin / Vaginal estrogen)
4. What device physically supports female stress incontinence? (Pads / Diapers / Pessaries / Urinary catheters / Sling procedures)
5. Which method can estimate postvoid residual (PVR) without catheter placement? (CT / MRI / Ultrasound / X-ray / Blood test)
